name: Sierra Pelada Natural Park Loop
name_original: Valdelamusa, Cortegana, Rosal de la Frontera – ruta circular
description: This challenging 78.3-mile road cycling route through Huelva province offers demanding climbs and scenic views of the Sierra de Huelva.
region_intro: Road cycling around Cortegana offers varied landscapes within the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park. The region features rolling hills, dense forests of cork and holm oaks, and fertile *dehesas*. Cyclists encounter challenging ascents and descents through valleys carved by rivers like the Múrtiga, with routes often traversing quiet, historic roads. This terrain provides a demanding yet rewarding environment for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes around Cortegana The most popular road cycling route is Circular route through Valdemusa, El Cerro de, a 54.2 miles (87.2 km) difficult trail that takes about 4 hours 13 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain through the region's characteristic rolling hills. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Valdelamusa, Cerro de Andévalo, Calañas – circular route, a moderate 38.1 miles (61.3 km) path. It winds through diverse landscapes, including *dehesas* and forested areas. Local road cyclists also love the Sierra Pelada Natural Park Loop, a 78.3 miles (126.0 km) trail leading through the natural park, often completed in about 6 hours 35 minutes. Road cycling around Cortegana is defined by rolling hills, dense oak forests, and river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging ascents. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 300 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Cortegana's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty of road cycling routes around Cortegana?
A: Road cycling around Cortegana offers a range of difficulties. Out of 36 available routes, 2 are considered easy, 14 are moderate, and 20 are difficult. The region is known for its challenging ascents and varied terrain, making it particularly rewarding for cyclists with good fitness.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Cortegana?
A: You can expect diverse terrain, including picturesque rolling hills, dense forests of cork and holm oaks, and fertile &lt;em&gt;dehesas&lt;/em&gt;. Routes often traverse valleys carved by rivers like the Múrtiga, winding through quiet white villages and along historical paths. While some local roads may have a less-than-perfect surface, they generally feature very light traffic.

Q: Are there challenging climbs for experienced road cyclists in Cortegana?
A: Yes, Cortegana is noted for some of the most challenging cycling climbs in the province of Huelva. Many routes require very good fitness due to significant elevation gains, offering a demanding yet rewarding experience for seasoned road cyclists. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24523900" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Marines Village – Linares de la Sierra loop from Almonaster - Cortegana&lt;/a&gt;, which features over 2,100 meters of elevation gain.

Q: What historical or cultural sights can I see while road cycling near Cortegana?
A: The region is rich in history. You can cycle past the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5300310" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cortegana Castle&lt;/a&gt;, a medieval fortress offering panoramic views. Other routes might take you through charming villages like Almonaster la Real, home to the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4550593" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mosque of Almonaster la Real&lt;/a&gt;, or past the Church of the Divine Savior in Cortegana itself.

Q: Are there any natural landmarks or scenic viewpoints along the road cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. The Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park offers stunning natural beauty. You might encounter the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7287170" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Waterfall in the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy views from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2894628" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alájar Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4184233" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arias Montano Peak&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4086077" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro de San Cristóbal&lt;/a&gt; also offer rewarding sights.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Cortegana?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991492186/circular-route-through-valdemusa-el-cerro-de" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular route through Valdemusa, El Cerro de&lt;/a&gt;, a challenging 87.2 km ride. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991500446/valdelamusa-cerro-de-andevalo-calanas-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdelamusa, Cerro de Andévalo, Calañas – circular route&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 61.3 km loop.

Q: Are there any easier road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: While many routes are challenging, there are a couple of easier options available. These routes typically feature less elevation gain and shorter distances, making them more accessible for those new to road cycling or looking for a more relaxed ride. You can find 2 easy routes among the 36 available in the area.

Q: When is the best time of year for road cycling in Cortegana?
A: The best time for road cycling in Cortegana is generally during spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, temperatures are milder and more pleasant for cycling, and the natural landscape is particularly vibrant. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be cooler and wetter, though still rideable on many days.

Q: What kind of wildlife and nature can I observe while cycling in the Sierra de Aracena?
A: The Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park is rich in biodiversity. As you cycle through cork oak and holm oak forests, you might spot various bird species, including raptors. The &lt;em&gt;dehesas&lt;/em&gt; are home to Iberian pigs, and the lush valleys and streams support diverse flora and fauna, offering a truly immersive natural experience.

Q: Are there options for less crowded road cycling routes in the Cortegana area?
A: Yes, many local roads around Cortegana experience very light traffic, especially those winding through the natural park and connecting smaller villages. This allows for a more peaceful and immersive cycling experience, away from busy main roads. Exploring routes that traverse historical paths between villages often leads to quieter stretches.

Q: Can I find cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes pass through or near charming villages like Cortegana, Almonaster la Real, and Linares de la Sierra. These villages typically offer cafes, bars, and restaurants where you can stop for refreshments, a meal, or to refill water bottles. Planning your route to include these stops can enhance your cycling experience.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Cortegana?
A: Road cyclists frequently praise the varied landscapes, from dense oak forests to rolling hills and river valleys. The challenging ascents and descents are a big draw for those seeking a demanding ride, while the quiet, historic roads and charming villages add to the unique cultural experience. The natural beauty within the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park is consistently highlighted as a major appeal.
tips: The flooded Valdelamusa quarry, also known as the Corta de los Confesionarios, is one of the most striking elements of the mining heritage of this district of Cortegana, in the province of Huelva.
Beautiful church in the border town of Rosal de la Frontera that together with the town's main square form a very picturesque architectural complex and an example of Huelva's interior.

This medieval fortress dates from the 13th century and its origin is still not completely clear, although it can be assumed that due to its shape and type of construction, its character was originally defensive. You can visit the inside and also the views from the path on the outside are very picturesque. For more info visit the official website (https://www.castillodecortegana.net/)

faqs.question: How challenging is the Valdelamusa, Cortegana, Rosal de la Frontera circular route for road cyclists?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a difficult road ride, spanning 126 kilometers with a significant elevation gain of approximately 1,600 meters. It demands very good fitness and typically takes around 6 hours and 36 minutes to complete. Be aware that some segments may be unpaved and difficult to ride, requiring caution.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily follows roads through the Sierra de Huelva, offering varied terrain. While much of it is likely paved, be prepared for some unpaved segments that can be challenging for road bikes. You'll cycle through picturesque cork oak and holm oak pastures, lush riverine vegetation, and extensively wooded landscapes, including chestnut groves in higher areas.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Valdelamusa, Cortegana, Rosal de la Frontera circular route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas aren't detailed, as a circular route connecting Valdelamusa, Cortegana, and Rosal de la Frontera, you can typically find parking in or near any of these towns to begin your ride. Look for public parking spaces in the village centers.

faqs.question: What natural features and landmarks will I see along the Valdelamusa, Cortegana, Rosal de la Frontera cycling route?
faqs.answer: You'll experience the beautiful landscapes of the Sierra de Huelva, part of the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park. Notable points include the Valdelamusa Water-Filled Quarry, the Church of San Isidro Labrador - Rosal de la Frontera, and Cortegana Castle. The route also offers views of traditional white towns and serene, green forests.

faqs.question: Is the Valdelamusa, Cortegana, Rosal de la Frontera circular route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As this is a demanding road cycling route, it is generally not recommended for cycling with dogs. The long distance, significant elevation, and potential for unpaved sections make it unsuitable for most canine companions.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Sierra de Huelva region can be quite warm in summer. Spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling, with milder weather and beautiful scenery. Winter can also be an option, but be prepared for cooler temperatures, especially at higher elevations.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay an entrance fee to cycle in the Paraje Natural Sierra Pelada y Rivera del Aserrador?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route through the Paraje Natural Sierra Pelada y Rivera del Aserrador. However, always respect local regulations, stay on marked paths, and practice Leave No Trace principles.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through charming traditional villages like Cortegana and Rosal de la Frontera. You'll find opportunities to stop for coffee or refreshments in local bars and cafes in these towns, allowing you to experience authentic Andalusian culture.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, while primarily its own distinct circular route, it briefly intersects with sections of other paths. You'll find small segments that overlap with the Transandalus, Carretera Camino de Gil Márquez, Camino Viejo de Aroche, and Valdelamusa trails.

faqs.question: What's an insider tip for tackling the Valdelamusa, Cortegana, Rosal de la Frontera circular route?
faqs.answer: Given that some segments of this route may be unpaved and difficult for typical racebikes, consider using a road bike with slightly wider tires (28mm or 30mm if your frame allows) for added comfort and grip. Alternatively, be prepared to navigate these sections with extra caution, especially on descents.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner road cyclists?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' and requires 'very good fitness' due to its 126 km distance and 1,600 meters of elevation gain. It is not recommended for beginner road cyclists.

waypoints.name: Church of San Isidro Labrador - Rosal de la Frontera
